text: The question in the title is deliberately provocative. It may seem a little bit weird at first because we have decades of security engineering wisdoms. We have formal methods, sandboxing, capability based systems, zero trust architectures. And yet, you look around, you open the news, and you see attacks against AI agents succeeding everywhere. And including jailbreaks, including prompt injections. And this is because AI agents remain fundamentally quite difficult to secure. Today, I want to explain why this is a matter of not insufficient effort, but rather this is a structural problem. It is because the assumptions that underpin modern security, they don't quite work for AI agents. I'll walk through what breaks, what the new threat landscape looks like, and where I see the genuine progress happening. In the entire history of computing as we know it today, there has never been a period of peace. From the earliest time-sharing systems to today's distributed infrastructure, the adversaries has always been omnipresent. Security has always been a sort of a, you know, cat and mouse game and arms race. But for the most of this history, the defensive side has made quite steady, measurable progress. It has learned from the mistakes of the past. So, why does it feel like that with AI, the things are getting kind of worse? And just think about the whole trajectory. The Morris worm in 1988 exploited the implicit trust between the computer systems. And it was like a a sort of a failure of the assumptions on the boundaries. And then the next generation of attacks, like Stuxnet or SolarWinds or Log4j or NPM supply chain attacks, they all exploited as explicit trust or implicit trust in software dependencies and the update mechanisms. And the sort of the adversity here exist in the infrastructure that we've chosen to trust. And a similar pattern is emerging in AI. We have those prompt injection based supply chain compromises, like as an example of C line incident that happened uh a bit ago. And there we had malicious instructions embedded in the repository that hijacked the agent's execution. And this context window is sort of like the new supply chain attacks on on AI systems. And at every stage, the security community learned and adapted. Ross Anderson, my PhD advisor and mentor, who sadly passed away last year, codified decades of those hard-won lessons into security engineering. That work and the broader research community gave us principles that generally improved the state of practice, right? We had memory safe languages that reduced the entire class of vulnerabilities. We had formal sandboxes that contained untrusted code. Least privilege became the status quo. It's like it's it's the architectural doctrine of how we build systems today. And the evidence is clear, security has been improving. The principle sort of worked, like the hard hard lessons that we learned. And those inherent principles that the field has internalized, I want to highlight sort of like a few that became somewhat important in how our posture developed. So, the very first one is once on the internet is there forever. And this underlying assumptions of data permanence meant that we designed for breach in in a very certain way. The second is that no adversary knows everything. This is quite fundamental for why defense in depth works. Precisely because the attacker's knowledge is always or almost always incomplete. And complexity long term is unmanageable. Today, we build our systems such that they remain simple enough to reason about them in and and the security properties that they carry around. And these are not sort of abstract axioms. They really shaped how the architecture decisions were made for the past three decades and they sort of helped. And then something changed. We build AI agents and the very structural assumptions underlying each of these principles kind of became to fail. And this is not sort of because you know, of engineering negligence or the practice is just bad. But this is just because the agents are designed to do things that the fundamental security principles that we we originally built and designed they were designed to prevent that. That tension is basically the core of this whole work of this whole talk. And many of the underlying assumptions that we relied on are as a result invalidated or at least partially invalidated. A very quick note about me. I've been breaking things since I was a kid. Now I am mostly trying to fix them. I did my PhD at the University of Cambridge with Professor Ross Anderson and then I spent a bit of time in Oxford. I spent a bit of time in Vector Institute and I spent quite a bit of time at Google DeepMind. Then I ran my own startups building secure AI and then I joined Meta. At Meta, my team works specifically on securing AI systems and that includes both attacking them and building defenses to make sure that they cannot be broken. Three questions structure this talk. First, do traditional security defenses transfer to AI agents? Second, what is the novel attack surface in the AI era? And third, what realistic grounds for optimism exist? And a spoiler alert for the first one, they don't really transfer to your agents. And let me lay out the comparison quite directly. The three paradigms that govern software security for the good decades are we either first trust the logic and isolate the data, and this covers input validation, type safety, control flow integrity. This is what we do today when we run, say, Linux binaries. This is us explicitly checking that the logic we are running, the programs we are running, are doing exactly what they're supposed to do, and then no data exists that will manipulate this logic and force the program to be something else. The second option is we don't trust the logic and we trust the data provenance. And this is covering things like information flow control, differential privacy, data isolation. And in these cases, although the programs can be anything, we make sure that whenever the data is used, it's used in a certain way such that we can quantify what the data is, how it's used, how much is being leaked, and so on. And finally, the third option is we trust neither. And this is probably covering different kinds of sandboxes with capabilities, with micro microkernel architectures. And this is sort of the worst-case scenario where we really don't know what to do. We can't provide you a better guarantee on security, so we hope for the best. We make this thing run and hope it will not escape a sandbox and cause damage. So, and on the right, here is all of the things that modern agents actually require to function. They execute arbitrary code. They establish persistent recurrent operations. They access external networks. They communicate with other agents. They take actions that have an actual real-world side effect. They carry a lot of biases in how they solve programs. It's very hard to even reason on what the normal execution of an agent is. And as you can imagine, none of those things align with the three options we outlined on the left. Those three paradigms don't quite apply to it. You cannot isolate this thing because the agent is required to have, you know, persistent regard to operations. You cannot really quantify data. It touches everything and touches it in ways that are quite often very hard to interpret. And they execute arbitrary code, something that we know is fundamentally almost impossible to reason about. So, yeah, things do not quite work in the same way as they did for software. And in multi-agent setups, things get even worse because you have agents interacting with other agents. The number of trust boundaries just skyrockets. Every agent must decide whether they want to trust another agent with the data they provide, with the actions they perform. And one compromised agent, as we know from the literature, can basically cause cascading effects and poison the entire system through context of memory. And worse, our long-running agents, they accumulate data. They accumulate attack surface. They accumulate permissions over time. And every tool call, every piece of data ingested is a potential injection point for the adversary. And as of today, no existing security framework is designed to really handle this. And yeah, it's it's really sort of like at every step of an agent execution, untrusted inputs can flow in. And it's quite hard to even establish what we're supposed to trust and at what point. Often you hear people describing human systems as multi-agent systems and saying, you know, we can build like relatively robust human systems and things work. And this comparison doesn't quite work because with the AI agents, you have agents working 24/7. They perform tasks. They function. and it's quite hard to even tell what you're expecting them to do. They poke with systems they poke with systems in ways that no human would ever do, and yeah, it's quite it's quite different. Let me tell you a story from the wild. And this is not a hypothetical. This happened with one of our startup sandboxes. And in what it is like to have compromises happen as a result of agent operations. We ran a benchmark to evaluate Openclaw. And the tasks that we designed ourselves were quite simple, and this is totally normal. This is normally what you would do with these agents. So, we ran a command that said, "Search for Python async best practices, and then create a GitHub issue summarizing what you found, right?" It's very straightforward. There is nothing complex about it. And the agent does exactly what it's told. It creates the issue with the findings. And there is nothing wrong here. The agent is pretty much just doing its job. And if you look at it, it it's totally normal. Like, everything looks legitimate. But look closer. See the highlighted word set. The agent is posting this particular text that you see on the screen publicly to GitHub. It's using the GitHub CLI to create the issue. And for us reading through it, it looks like just the text that the agent output. However, because of the way Openclaw was implemented at the time that we were running this, what actually would happen inside is it would take all of this command, and when it issues the exact tool call, this would be passed into the bash environment. For those of us who are old enough to understand how bash works and familiar with all of the weird semantics inside, we know that the backticks are actually used to execute command execute contents. You pass in the commands inside of these backticks. And when this particular text is passed to into the bash environment, what actually happens is bash sees, "Okay, there is a backtick inside. That means the set is actually not text. This is actually a command that is passed into it." When you execute set on top of your box, what actually happens is set command normally sets environment variables. If you pass it nothing, it lists all of the environment variables and the contents of such environment variables that it currently has into in your terminal. And what happens when you have a command like this is that it takes the backtick set and replaces it with pretty much all of the secrets that you have, all of the credentials that you currently have in your system. And as you can imagine, this is pretty bad because you are uploading all of your secrets, all of your operational secrets and credentials onto publicly readable repository on GitHub. Here is what actually happened. The agent leaked its authentication token in the process of interacting with GitHub. The attacker was monitoring GitHub for exactly these kinds of leaked credentials from AI agents because they knew that often people run benchmarks and or their agents with actual life credentials, with access to the systems. The attacker then used the token to inject a prompt into the agent's environment and got it to download the remote access Trojan. And that way they established a persistent connection to the command and control center. And it's a fully automated supply chain compromise starting from our benchmark test where we run our agent on top of our harness with our own prompts, like and everything looked benign by itself. And in this particular setting, the agent didn't even do anything wrong. It's just the infrastructure around the agent failed and interpreted the command that the agent sorry, the data that the agent passed in as a command. And it followed, the agent actually followed all of its instructions correctly. The failure itself is the systemic. It's the excessive ambient authority. It's insufficient credential isolation. The agent here was conduit. It's It's not the vulnerability. One may argue that this just reflects an immature agent implementation, but actually this challenge today pretty much extends to modern well-engineered agents as well. The fundamental issue itself is really architecture. It's not not implementation. And this isn't just about benchmarks with poor security hygiene. Modern production agents and the ones we're deploying today, they're also vulnerable. So, note how in the previous example of the sandbox compromise, we really did not have any adversaries involved until the later stages. So, the agent made a mistake itself. However, we also know that we can force the agents to make mistakes. We can attack the agents and get them to perform the actions that normally they wouldn't be performing. And this is a central challenge, and I really want to be precise about why this is structural. In traditional security, we have quite a cardinal rule. Separate the control flow from data. We never execute data. Never allow untrusted inputs to influence the control logic of your program. And this principle itself eliminated a large class of different attacks we have. Right? For example, it eliminated SQL injections. It eliminated cross-site scripting. It eliminated buffer overflows and entire vulnerability classes themselves, the the the the exploits that we have to deal with. But consider AI and how users interact with the AI systems. So, for example, when I say, "Find my manager's email and follow the steps inside of it. In security terms, this is equivalent to load instruction for external untrusted data source and execute them. And this is exactly what we spent 30 years preventing. And the paradox here is that the user actually wants these behaviors. This is truly where the value proposition for AI systems lie. They are very good at dealing with uncertainty. You don't actually need to codify quite a lot of things that normally you have to do. And unfortunately, the normal security semantics that we're used to, they don't quite apply because you can no longer separate away the control and data flows. And at the same time, more recently, we also have agents execute code and arbitrary code as well. And this makes quite like quite a difference because we know that fundamentally from the complexity theory, that in generality, we cannot reason about the code. We have to in order for us to understand what the code does, we actually have to run it. And we kind of sort of moved from the world where in the past we needed to have, you know, CVEs, zero days to compromise machines to now actually just submitting exploits that just require convincing sentences for the model to run whereby it can run arbitrary code on your machine from just untrusted data whatsoever. And this can be as simple as, you know, sending an email to an agent that just monitors your mailbox and at the same time has access to your banking information because it needs to make bookings for you. And yeah, it seems like as of today, we can't really reason about how to run this in a in a generally secure way. And it actually gets somewhat worse because there is a number of papers showing strong attacks against agents with defenses in place as well. Some of it we contributed to in our previous startup as you see on the left and some of this are coming out of Meta where we show that AI can be used to automate attacks against other AI systems and those attacks are actually very very efficient. The attacker moves second on the left shows adaptive attacks that basically bypass all of the known defenses whereas the RL as a hammer paper on the right shows that you can train RL agents to generate prompt injections. And the attacker here is no longer just a human with a keyboard where they need to sort of, you know, type out sentences. It's another AI with unlimited patience that sort of performs interactions with the other agents. And the empirical results are quite quite stark. Here the green bars show static non-adaptive attacks and this is basically the kind used in most of the evaluations nowadays. And there are defenses that appear effective against this. And the brown bars represent the automated adaptive attacks and you can see there that success rates reach quite high and against pretty much different classes of defenses from prompting based to training based to filtering. And it's it's it's a big deal. None none of the 12 defenses that were evaluated really survived strong adaptive adversaries. And the vulnerability is there and we know that those agents can be manipulated. So in all of the settings where you can send a message to a model, you can get some of your inputs into the model. As of today, it seems that in generality we don't really know how to solve the fundamental agent issues. However, I do want to say that, you know, that this story is not without hope. There are some sort of concrete measurable reasons for optimism. And let me describe what what is actually working. First, and somewhat surprising, is that alignment is producing real results. Models today are substantially more robust than they were 18 months ago, 12 months ago, actually 6 months ago. And the cost of finding successful jailbreaks and prompt injections has increased from seconds to hours. And the number of queries required has grown quite a bit as well from, you know, tens or 20 in the past to thousands nowadays. And I think, crucially, a lot of the successful attacks are actually quite unstable. So, they work once and then they fail and then they fail again and they like you have to keep on trying. And this instability actually helps detection efforts quite a bit. And the other thing is we've had quite a lot of methodological innovation that enabled provenance and governance in quite a fine-grained ways for AI systems. You can actually enforce data flow separation where it's applicable, like control data flow separation and in the way we were saying is quite hard to do for AI systems. We can actually track what data influence decisions. We can build somewhat auditable control flow graphs in agent execution. And this is quite a big deal. And at the same time, it's worth noting that since those those agents now run arbitrary code, we do have to deal with fundamental limitations to to to computation itself, right? So, halting problem applies, confused deputy problem will apply. Meaning in in general, we will not be able to solve all classes of issues. And at the same time, we can solve a lot of those issues. And yeah, so over the next couple of years, I'm sure we'll we'll improve our defensive systems. At the same time, we are already at a point where we're building meaningful mechanisms to enforce security policies of different choice. To summarize, the old principles don't really transfer. We really need new security semantics for AI. It's generally unsolved, and you should really be skeptical of anyone claiming otherwise. It's exciting because we are the people who are supposed to solve the security of the future. And at the same time, I think we're getting hold of it. The indirect prompt injections are becoming more manageable. They're not solved as of yet, but the bar is rising, but we can already eliminate a large class of attacks of different attacks. Data-dependent things on untrusted inputs are obviously the the hard frontier in all of this. And for many of these things, since they're quite fundamentally unsolvable, enforcement must happen at the system level. We are building mechanisms for this, and it's likely not have going to happen inside of the model. But yeah, I'm optimistic. I think we're quite close to solving a lot a lot of AI security issues. Thanks for tuning in. I hope you enjoyed the talk.
